top_candidates.reasoning: The 'small water-pond' / 'flood' structural language is verbatim from yāvān artha udapāne sarvataḥ samplutodake; the rule-vs-principle and 'practitioner who has reached the principle' framing maps directly onto brāhmaṇasya vijānataḥ; the falsifiability clause about not abandoning the linter on 'I have internalized the principles' grounds maps onto Shankara's gloss that the unrealized still needs the well.

BG 2.52 (yadā te moha-kalilaṁ buddhir vyatitariṣyati / tadā gantāsi nirvedaṁ śrotavyasya śrutasya ca) speaks to the seeker who, having crossed the thicket of delusion, becomes indifferent to what is to be heard and what has been heard — a related but more interior framing than the well/flood structure. Distant possibility because of the 'no longer needs the cookbook' shape, but the analog's well/flood metaphor is too specific for this candidate.

Distant possibility because 'the rule dissolves into the practitioner' has a faint echo of sarva-dharmān parityajya ('abandoning all dharmas'). But 18.66 is about ultimate surrender, not about the realized one's relation to scripture — and the engineering layer's emphasis on artifact-fluency rather than transcendence rules this out.
